risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After confirming the diagnosis of uncomplicated AA by a CT scan, patients will be randomized by a closed envelope method either to undergo appendectomy or to receive antibiotic therapy with intravenous ertapenem. The randomization is performed in 1:1 equal allocation ratio. The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This describes a randomized allocation procedure using opaque, sealed, sequentially numbered envelopes opened in order, which indicates a systematic random process. While the exact sequence-generation algorithm is not named, the paper explicitly reports randomization with sufficient detail and context clues to support a truly random allocation sequence, so Low risk is appropriate under the permissive criteria.

Quote: "The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals... To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelope (SNOSE) system, which prevents the enrolling surgeon from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial to compare antibiotic therapy (intravenous ertapenem) with emergency appendectomy" and "The best design for a therapeutic trial is a randomized placebo-controlled, double-blind study, but with the interventions used in the APPAC trial the concealment would not be possible and therefore a randomized open design was chosen." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were not blinded in this open-label trial, and knowledge of assignment could plausibly affect several outcomes judged in this review (e.g. pain scores, length of hospital stay, sick leave, treatment decisions/crossover, and complications), so the study is at high risk of performance bias overall.

Summary: The trial was explicitly "open label" and the outcomes were assessed by a mix of patient self-report and clinician judgement/record review. The protocol states: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label... trial" and follow-up was by "phone interview" with "pain score (VAS), possible additional need for sick leave, wound infections and recurrent AA" recorded. It also states recurrent AA was "diagnosed on a clinical basis" and wound infection was "diagnosed by a surgeon or positive bacterial culture." Comment: Because assessors were not blinded and several outcomes were subjective or judgement-dependent (pain, sick leave, recurrent appendicitis diagnosed clinically, wound infection assessed by surgeon), knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement.

Quote: "With an estimated 10 percent of the trial patients lost to follow-up, a maximum of 610 patients will be enrolled." and "The main analyses will be based on the intention-to-treat principle, but both intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses will be performed." Comment: The protocol anticipated some loss to follow-up but only about 10%, which is a reasonable level of attrition, and it states that the main analysis will be intention-to-treat. There is no signal here of imbalanced or outcome-related dropout that would be likely to bias results.
